commit.message:
8270068: Add check/verfication for impossible constraints

This patch introduces elementary validation for `@IR(failOn)` and
`@IR(counts)` annotations.  Specifically, if `CompilePhaseIRRuleBuilder`
notices that either (1) the same node appears in both the `failOn` and
`counts` lists (with a non-zero expected count) or (2) if it appears
more than once in the `counts` list with incompatible counts or (3) if
it appears in an equality constraint (e.g. "6") as well as an inequality
constraint (e.g. ">4"), then the test framework throws an exception.
The last criteria (=6 AND >4) is flagged since it is likely an oversight
by the test author, even though the constraints are satisfiable.

When checking whether the count is valid, it is insufficient to simply
ensure that the same node identifier appears at most once in the
`counts` list, since there are several valid cases like the constraint
`@IR(counts = {IRNode.MAX_L, "> 0", IRNode.MAX_L, "<= 2"})` in
TestLoopLimitSubtractionsCollapse.java when the node may appear more
than once.  Consequently, we need some form of a satisfiability check.
To keep the check fast, the code uses bitvectors, but as a result, it is
only able to check counts upto 63, which is likely to be the common case
for our IR tests.

As a result, incompatible counts larger than 63 are not flagged, but the
test framework prints a warning in such cases.  While it is possible to
use either arbitrary-precision integers or logic that checks for
compatibility between comparison operators, both these approaches are
likely to be slower than native bitvectors, so we forego the uncommon
case of testing counts that are larger than 63.
